items.title: Bitcoin's biggest advocate, Michael Saylor, says new plan to clean up the blockchain is 'a bad idea'
items.description: Michael Saylor argues that a new proposal, BIP-110, to temporarily block "spam" data from the Bitcoin blockchain would undermine the network's neutrality and create a dangerous precedent for censorship.

items.title: AI is destroying the internet. Math is our only hope.
items.description: The rise of autonomous AI agents necessitates the utilization of zero-knowledge proofs, argues Brian Trunzo, chief growth officer at Succinct Labs.

items.title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
items.description: The GENIUS Act has hit its first anniversary without U.S. regulators yet meeting deadlines to write regulations, though rules will be in full effect by July 2028.

items.title: Kraken says simpler options can unlock crypto's next derivatives market
items.description: The Wyoming-based firm launched USD-settled bitcoin and ether options, saying product design, not demand, has held back adoption.

items.title: Bitcoin’s quantum problem gets a recovery tool, but not for Satoshi’s 1.1 million coins
items.description: Project Eleven says it has funded a proof that lets a wallet's own key-derivation path stand in as ownership after quantum computers can forge its signatures. It runs in 243 milliseconds on a laptop.

items.title: Inside Zcash's new node that targets Visa-scale privacy at 50,000 transactions per second
items.description: The new Zakura client is the first live piece of a plan to take Zcash from roughly one private transaction per second to payment-network scale.

items.title: France orders country's internet service providers to block Polymarket
items.description: The regulator cited concerns over addictive mechanics, a lack of self-exclusion tools, and a high volume of French users bypassing previous financial restrictions.

items.title: Crypto executives say digital native generations may never need a bank account
items.description: Steakhouse Financial co-founder Adrian Cachinero says digital-native generations may rely less on banks, while Binance says younger users are already driving crypto adoption in emerging markets.

items.title: Here’s what happened in crypto today
items.description: Need to know what happened in crypto today? Here is the latest news on daily trends and events impacting Bitcoin price, blockchain, DeFi, Web3 and crypto regulation.

items.title: US agencies miss GENIUS Act deadline for final stablecoin rules
items.description: US regulators failed to finalize implementing regulations by the GENIUS Act’s one-year deadline, issuing 10 proposed rules instead.

items.title: Upbit parent Dunamu faces South Korean regulator sanctions process: Report
items.description: Korea’s Virtual Asset User Protection Act lacks explicit sanctions provisions for hacking and similar incidents, clouding the scope of penalties facing the Upbit operator Dunamu.

items.title: Electronic Transactions Association CEO Expecting More Partnerships with Bitcoin Startups
items.description: Electronic Transactions Association (ETA) CEO, Jason Oxman, indicated that members of his organization might start recognizing Bitcoin’s disruptive potential, suggesting that this might lead to more partnerships between traditional electronic payment providers and Bitcoin startups.

items.title: Kaspersky identifies malware framework targeting crypto investors
items.description: Cybersecurity company Kaspersky said a newly identified malware framework is targeting cryptocurrency investors through social engineering tactics and trojanized GitHub apps.

items.title: French gambling regulator orders ISPs to block Polymarket
items.description: Autorité nationale des jeux has ordered France’s internet service providers to geoblock Polymarket, citing illegal gambling and market manipulation concerns.

items.title: FTX to distribute $900M to creditors in fifth payment round
items.description: The FTX Recovery Trust and company have distributed about $10 billion since the exchange filed for bankruptcy in November 2022, leaving users cut off from their funds.

items.title: Galaxy lands 15-year Texas Tech stadium naming rights deal
items.description: Galaxy Digital will rename Texas Tech’s football stadium under a 15-year agreement, expanding its West Texas presence as the state attracts growing crypto investment.

items.title: U.S. says it targeted Iranian forces after attacks that killed two American service members
items.description: Centcom says it hit Iran's coastal surveillance and air defense facilities a day after two U.S. troops were killed in an Iranian strike on Jordan.

items.title: American Airlines CEO lays out his vision to close a more than $3 billion profit gap
items.description: The carrier is working on improving reliability, investing in more premium seats and lounges, and considering Boeing and Airbus for a new wide-body plane order.

items.title: Cybersecurity risks posed by over-the-air tech in autos has analysts concerned
items.description: The automotive industry's increasing use of over-the-air technology makes it more susceptible to cyberattacks, analysts say.

items.title: Housing costs is the top political issue for young voters, CNBC survey finds
items.description: The cost of housing is the top priority for voters ages 18 to 34, besting food and healthcare cost concerns, the CNBC All-America Economic Survey found.

items.title: The market for psychedelic drugs to treat mental health conditions gets a big buy signal
items.description: Eli Lilly's acquisition of psychedelic drug maker AtaiBeckley extends Big Pharma's embrace of a stigmatized class of medications for treating mental health.

items.title: Kalshi adds 3 million new users as prediction market capitalizes on the World Cup
items.description: The prediction market has launched some ads featuring soccer players, as well as formed a partnership to display its brand in more places during the tournament.

items.title: Top Wall Street analysts suggest these 3 dividend stocks for steady income
items.description: Here are three dividend-paying stocks that are highlighted by Wall Street's top pros, as tracked by TipRanks.

items.title: Taylor Farms recalls iceberg lettuce in 27 states due to cyclosporiasis outbreak
items.description: Taylor Farms is recalling iceberg lettuce in 27 states because of the potential that the greens could be contaminated with cyclosporiasis.

items.title: Aevo Brings One-Tap Protected Perps to Mobile With PERPS+ Launch

items.title: How Maga fell hard for the gender binary

items.title: Why ‘rightwing’ is no longer such a dirty word in Britain
items.description: When words like fascist and Nazi are thrown around, being merely conservative seems mild by comparison

items.title: It’s time for Europe to embrace air conditioning
items.description: More frequent hot summers will harm productivity and claim lives

items.title: Luxury groups face inventory squeeze under EU destruction ban
items.description: Ban particularly sensitive for industry that keeps products desirable by maintaining their scarcity

items.title: US day traders flock to ‘the most dangerous product in crypto’
items.description: Trump administration has opened American markets to highly leveraged perpetual futures

items.transcript_reason:
Could not retrieve a transcript for the video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uot-zXCpyBE! This is most likely caused by:

YouTube is blocking requests from your IP. This usually is due to one of the following reasons:
- You have done too many requests and your IP has been blocked by YouTube
- You are doing requests from an IP belonging to a cloud provider (like AWS, Google Cloud Platform, Azure, etc.). Unfortunately, most IPs from cloud providers are blocked by YouTube.

There are two things you can do to work around this:
1. Use proxies to hide your IP address, as explained in the "Working around IP bans" section of the README (https://github.com/jdepoix/youtube-transcript-api?tab=readme-ov-file#working-around-ip-bans-requestblocked-or-ipblocked-exception).
2. (NOT RECOMMENDED) If you authenticate your requests using cookies, you will be able to continue doing requests for a while. However, YouTube will eventually permanently ban the account that you have used to authenticate with! So only do this if you don't mind your account being banned!

If you are sure that the described cause is not responsible for this error and that a transcript should be retrievable, please create an issue at https://github.com/jdepoix/youtube-transcript-api/issues. Please add which version of youtube_transcript_api you are using and provide the information needed to replicate the error. Also make sure that there are no open issues which already describe your problem!

items.title: Can former employers withhold money from your 401(k) when you’re laid off?
items.description: There are two main ways to move money from your workplace retirement plan when you leave your job. One can cost you.

items.title: I racked up $35,000 in credit-card debt. Should I file for bankruptcy?
items.description: “I’m also considering working with a credit-counseling agency or enrolling in a hardship program.”

items.title: Can the ‘Magnificent Seven’ save a stock market that might be doomed without them?
items.description: Apple, Amazon, Nvidia and peers are quietly coming back to life, and that could easily jolt this sleepy stock market back into gear.

items.title: ‘I am at a loss’: My brother is dying. We are not close. What responsibility do I have to help him?
items.description: “He is now no longer able to think clearly or communicate his wishes.”

items.title: My husband and I are retired. Our financial adviser, who is in his 30s, called us ‘you guyses.’ Is that unprofessional?
items.description: “I’m wondering whether I should ask to work with someone closer to our generation.”

items.title: ‘Respect is important’: If my financial adviser said, ‘Hey,’ my wife and I would walk out. Where do you draw the line?
items.description: “I met with a financial representative who was not aware that AT&T was the shortened form of American Telephone and Telegraph.”

items.title: ‘They are relentless’: A scammer is trying to change the email on my empty Robinhood account. What do they have to gain?
items.description: “I’ve just been ignoring their attempts.”

items.title: Netflix is spending big money on sports. Is the company making the right bets?
items.description: The streaming service says live programming helps draw in new subscribers, but investors have become disillusioned with Netflix’s broader engagement trends.

items.title: Silicon shadows: inside the black market for AI chips | FT Film

YouTube is blocking requests from your IP. This usually is due to one of the following reasons:
- You have done too many requests and your IP has been blocked by YouTube
- You are doing requests from an IP belonging to a cloud provider (like AWS, Google Cloud Platform, Azure, etc.). Unfortunately, most IPs from cloud providers are blocked by YouTube.

There are two things you can do to work around this:
1. Use proxies to hide your IP address, as explained in the "Working around IP bans" section of the README (https://github.com/jdepoix/youtube-transcript-api?tab=readme-ov-file#working-around-ip-bans-requestblocked-or-ipblocked-exception).
2. (NOT RECOMMENDED) If you authenticate your requests using cookies, you will be able to continue doing requests for a while. However, YouTube will eventually permanently ban the account that you have used to authenticate with! So only do this if you don't mind your account being banned!

If you are sure that the described cause is not responsible for this error and that a transcript should be retrievable, please create an issue at https://github.com/jdepoix/youtube-transcript-api/issues. Please add which version of youtube_transcript_api you are using and provide the information needed to replicate the error. Also make sure that there are no open issues which already describe your problem!

items.title: UK must focus on insiders vs outsiders not north vs south
items.description: Economists and politicians want to boost growth, but it must include people on the periphery of hotspots

items.title: EU faces crumbling support for new Russia sanctions
items.description: National capitals refuse to support measures that could hurt big companies

items.title: Burnham to maintain ban on North Sea exploration licences
items.description: Decision criticised by oil and gas industry as well as trade union

items.title: India’s rupee problem has been misdiagnosed
items.description: Currency weakness reflects subdued private investment and uncertainty over the business climate

items.title: Burnham should pursue a devolution ‘big bang’
items.description: If done right, radical decentralisation could boost UK productivity growth and living standards

items.title: UK steel quota concessions to seal India deal pose risk to Welsh plant, say insiders
items.description: Union leaders say quotas for galvanised steel particularly harmful for Tata Steel UK’s Llanwern plant in Newport

items.title: How will the ECB respond to the latest rise in oil prices?
items.description: Market Questions is the FT’s guide to the week ahead

items.title: China’s Jingye demands payout over British Steel nationalisation
items.description: UK government accused of disregarding group’s investment and offering ‘almost zero compensation’

items.title: The Case of the 3:30 Emails
items.description: Plus: Are you really paying attention to me on Zoom?

items.title: Step Into the ‘Zone of Genius’ (Before A.I. Takes Your Job)
items.description: A decades-old self-help concept is gaining new purchase among people searching for meaningful work in the age of artificial intelligence.

items.title: The ‘Bad Blood’ Between Polymarket’s Shayne Coplan and Kalshi’s Tarek Mansour
items.description: Shayne Coplan of Polymarket and Tarek Mansour of Kalshi are young billionaires intent on dominating prediction markets. It’s more than a battle. It’s “bad blood.”

items.title: Adidas Hopes Its World Cup Moment Can Drive U.S. Sales
items.description: Nearly everything on the field on Sunday will bear the German company’s logo. Bjorn Gulden, the chief executive, says he “couldn’t have scripted it better.”

items.title: Madison Square Garden Sues Wired Magazine Over L.G.B.T.Q. Tracking Report
items.description: The Manhattan venue claims a July article from the technology magazine falsely implied that it tracked information about some celebrities’ sexual orientation for “discriminatory purposes.”

items.title: America Caught World Cup Fever. His Job Is to Capitalize on It.
items.description: Don Garber, the M.L.S. commissioner, has been planning for this moment for eight years.

items.title: World Cup Final Drives Billions in Bets on Kalshi and Polymarket
items.description: The soccer tournament has helped propel the prediction markets to new user records, creating what may be the largest gambling event in history.

items.title: Robinhood’s Chief on Trump Accounts and Why Trading Isn’t Necessarily Gambling
items.description: Vlad Tenev, the chief executive of the digital brokerage Robinhood, is trying to win over the next generation of Americans with Trump accounts.
